1: wayyēdaʿ ʾādām ʾet-ḥawwāh: "Adam knew Eve his wife." Yādaʿ (H3045) is the standard Hebrew verb for intimate covenantal knowing; it covers experiential, relational, and sexual knowledge in a single word that the English "knew" or the MKT's "made love" must unpack contextually. The name Qayin (Cain) is explained by Eve's wordplay: qānîtî ʾîš ʾet-YHWH — "I have acquired a man with/by YHWH." The verb qānāh (H7069) means to acquire, purchase, or create; Eve may be expressing gratitude for divine gift or, more boldly, a claim that she has participated in divine creation. The etymology is embedded in the name — the narrator preserves the theological self-understanding of the character in the very word that names him.
2: hābel rōʿēh ṣōʾn wĕqayin hāyāh ʿōbēd ʾădāmāh: Abel is a keeper of flocks; Cain is a tiller of the ground. The name Hābel (Abel, H1893) means breath, vapor, or vanity — the same word that opens Ecclesiastes ("Vanity of vanities, all is hābel"). Whether this reflects retrospective naming or anticipates his early death is disputed, but the text allows the association. The two vocations divide the brothers by their relation to the ground: Cain works the cursed earth of Gen 3:17; Abel tends animals that graze above it.
3: miqṣēh yāmîm ... minḥāh laYHWH: "In the course of time Cain brought from the fruit of the ground an offering to YHWH." Minḥāh (H4503) is the standard word for a tribute or grain offering — used in Levitical law for non-animal offerings but also generically for any gift presented to a superior. The phrase does not specify what was wrong with Cain's offering at this stage; the text delays explanation until v4-5. Miqṣēh yāmîm (literally "from the end of days") indicates a recurring or periodic occasion, not a one-time event.
4: wĕhābel hēbîʾ gam-hûʾ mibĕkōrôt ṣōʾnô ûmēḥelbêhen: "Abel also brought from the firstborn of his flock and from their fat portions." Two qualifiers distinguish Abel's offering: (1) bĕkōrôt (firstborn/firstlings) — the first and therefore best of the flock, with covenantal priority (cf. Exod 13:2; Deut 15:19-20); (2) ḥelĕbêhen (fat portions) — the choicest, most valued part, reserved for God in later Levitical law (Lev 3:16). The text makes no complaint about Cain's species of offering; the contrast is quality and priority of what each brought. Wayyišaʿ (looked with favor) — šāʿāh (H8159) means to gaze intently at or regard approvingly, implying a divine assessment, not merely emotional preference.
5: wĕʾel-qayin wĕʾel-minḥātô lōʾ šāʿāh: "But to Cain and his offering he did not look with favor." The Hebrew construction pairs the person and the offering under a single verb, suggesting a unity: it is Cain-and-his-offering as a composite that is refused. Wayyiḥar lĕqayin mĕʾōd (Cain was very angry) — ḥārāh (H2734) is a term for burning anger; mĕʾōd (exceedingly) intensifies it. Wayyippĕlû pānāyw (his face fell/was downcast) — the face as the site of honor and shame; a fallen face signals both grief and indignation. The outward sign precedes the inner murderous intent that God immediately addresses.
6: wayyōʾmer YHWH ʾel-qayin lāmmāh ḥārāh lāk wĕlāmmāh nāpĕlû pānêkā: "Why are you angry, and why has your face fallen?" The divine interrogation uses the same terms from v5: ḥārāh (burning anger) and nāpĕlû pānêkā (fallen face). God's question is not ignorant inquiry — the Hebrew interrogative lāmmāh (why?) functions as a challenge that calls for self-examination, not mere information. The pattern is identical to 3:9 ("Where are you?"): God asks not because he lacks the answer but because he gives the human space to answer honestly before pronouncing judgment.
7:
ḥaṭṭāʾt rōbēṣ: "Sin is crouching." Ḥaṭṭāʾt (H2403) is the standard word for sin and also for the sin-offering that makes atonement for it — the word carries both the offense and its remedy in a single term. Here it is personified as rōbēṣ (H7257, crouching/lurking) — the participial form of a verb that describes an animal crouched in wait. The Akkadian cognate rābiṣu denotes a guardian demon that lurks at doorways; the Mesopotamian background gives the image supernatural menace without requiring a direct demon reference.

Wĕʾēlêkā tĕšûqātô wĕʾattāh timšol-bô: "Its desire is for you, but you must rule over it." Tĕšûqāh (H8669, desire/longing) is the same word used of the woman's desire for her husband in 3:16, creating a deliberate echo: the disordered desire that entered human relationships in ch. 3 now appears as the character of sin itself. Māšal (H4910, rule/have dominion) repeats the language of 1:26-28; Cain is told he can do what Adam failed to do — but only if he acts rightly.
8: wayyāqom qayin ʾel-hābel ʾāḥîw wayyahargēhû: "Cain rose against his brother Abel and killed him." The Hebrew is stark and unadorned — no internal monologue, no plan, no narrative delay. Wayyāqom (rose up/arose) is a verb of sudden violent action; hārag (H2026, killed) is unambiguous — it is murder, not manslaughter. The narrator frames the act by repeating ʾāḥîw (his brother) at the end: "he killed him — his brother." The repeated kinship term underscores the covenant violation: the first murder is also the first fratricide.
9: ʾî hābel ʾāḥîkā: "Where is Abel your brother?" Again the divine question with implied omniscience — God already knows. Cain's answer lōʾ yādaʿtî (I do not know) uses the same verb yādaʿ from v1 — the "knowing" that opens this chapter (Adam knew Eve; God knows where Abel is) is now refused by Cain. Hăšōmēr ʾāḥî ʾānōkî: "Am I my brother's keeper?" The word šōmēr (keeper/guardian) is used in Num 3:38 for those who guard the tabernacle — ironically, Cain is precisely his brother's designated guardian in the covenantal sibling order he has violated.
10: qôl dĕmê ʾāḥîkā ṣōʿăqîm ʾēlay min-hāʾădāmāh: "The voice of your brother's blood is crying out to me from the ground." Dāmîm (bloods) is plural — the plural of blood often denotes bloodguilt or shed blood specifically. Ṣāʿaq (H6817, to cry out/appeal) is a judicial term — it denotes the cry of a victim appealing to a judge or avenger (cf. Exod 22:23; Deut 22:27). The ground itself becomes a witness and complainant; the inanimate creation testifies against the murderer. The language anticipates the concept of the "avenger of blood" (gōʾēl haddām) that becomes formalized in Num 35.
11: ʾārûr ʾattāh min-hāʾădāmāh: "Cursed are you from the ground, which has opened its mouth to receive your brother's blood from your hand." Cain is cursed min-hāʾădāmāh (from/by the ground) — not just expelled from it, but alienated from the very element that was his livelihood. The ground is personified as having "opened its mouth" to receive the blood, making it both victim and witness. The curse escalates from Gen 3:17: there the ground was cursed for Adam's sake; here Cain is cursed from the ground — a more direct and personal alienation.
12: nāʿ wānād tihyeh bāʾāreṣ: "A restless wanderer you will be on the earth." Nāʿ wānād is a word-pair — nûaʿ (H5128, to shake/stagger/wander) + nûd (H5110, to nod/wander/show sympathy) — combining two words for aimless motion. The phrase occurs only here and in v14; it is the deliberate reversal of Cain's identity as a tiller of the ground (ʿōbēd ʾădāmāh, v2). The one who cultivated the earth is now excluded from cultivating it; the agricultural man becomes the homeless wanderer.
13: gādôl ʿăwōnî miṇṇĕśōʾ: "My punishment is greater than I can bear" — or, "My iniquity is too great to be forgiven." ʿĀwōn (H5771) is ambiguous: it means both the sinful act and the punishment/consequence it brings. Miṇṇĕśōʾ (from being carried/lifted) can mean "too great to carry" (unbearable punishment) or "too great to be lifted off" (unforgivable guilt). The translation "bear" collapses both; the Hebrew preserves the ambiguity intentionally. Cain's complaint is simultaneously a recognition of guilt and a cry of self-pity — he describes the consequences accurately but shows no repentance.
14: ûmippānêkā ʾessātēr: "And from your face I will be hidden." Pānîm (face) — the divine countenance is the source of covenant blessing (Num 6:25-26; Ps 31:16). To be hidden from God's face is to lose covenant standing — this is the worst of the consequences Cain lists. The phrase connects to Gen 3:8 ("they hid themselves from the face of YHWH God") but there the hiding was the humans' action; here it is the structural consequence of the curse. The "land of Nod" (ʾereṣ nôd, v16) is named from the same root as nûd (wandering) — it is literally "the land of wandering."
15: lākēn kol-hōrēg qayin šibʿātayim yuqqām: "Therefore, anyone who kills Cain will suffer vengeance sevenfold." Šibʿātayim (sevenfold) — the number seven in biblical idiom connotes completeness; sevenfold vengeance is total divine protection. The ʾôt (H226, mark/sign) placed on Cain is the same word used for covenantal signs: rainbow (Gen 9:12), circumcision (Gen 17:11), blood on doorposts (Exod 12:13). The mark is not a brand of shame but a protective sign; its nature is not described. YHWH's protection of the murderer demonstrates the principle of common grace: God preserves fallen humanity to prevent self-annihilation before the purposes of redemption are accomplished.
16: wayyēṣēʾ qayin millipnê YHWH wayyēšeb bĕʾereṣ-nôd qidmat-ʿēden: "Cain went out from the presence of YHWH and settled in the land of Nod, east of Eden." Qidmat-ʿēden (east of Eden) echoes the expulsion direction of 3:24 (the cherubim are placed east of the garden). East in Genesis is the direction of exile and departure from the divine presence (cf. also Lot's departure toward Sodom, 13:11-12). The land of Nod is not a geographical region on any ancient map; its name (nûd, wandering) identifies it as the territory of homelessness itself — a theological rather than cartographic location.
17: wayyiben qayin ʿîr: "Cain built a city." The irony is pointed: the restless wanderer under a curse of displacement immediately founds a city — a permanent human settlement. ʿÎr (H5892, city/town) is the first city in Scripture, built by the first murderer. The name ḥănôk (Enoch) means "dedication" or "initiation" — Cain dedicates the first built environment to his son's name, not to YHWH's. This contrasts with the later calling on YHWH's name in v26.
18: wayyiwwālēd lĕḥănôk ʾet-ʿîrād: "To Enoch was born Irad." The Cainite genealogy (vv17-24) runs seven generations from Adam (Adam → Cain → Enoch → Irad → Mehujael → Methushael → Lamech). The names in the Cainite line have parallels in the Sethite line of ch. 5 (Enoch/Enoch, Lamech/Lamech, Methushael/Methuselah), suggesting either shared naming traditions, deliberate contrasts, or an ancient compiler's parallel arrangement of two traditions. The structural parallelism does not indicate that the lines are identical — the narrator distinguishes them clearly by lineage and outcome.
19: wayyiqqaḥ lô lemek šĕtê nāšîm: "Lamech took two wives." This is the first occurrence of polygamy in Scripture. The narrator introduces it without explicit commentary, but its place in the Cainite genealogy — the line marked by murder, wandering, and divine estrangement — gives it a negative context. The verb lāqaḥ (H3947, to take/acquire) is the standard verb for taking a wife in the patriarchal narrative; the specification of two signals a departure from the one-flesh pattern of Gen 2:24.
20: ʾābî yōšēb ʾōhel ûmiqneh: "The father of those who dwell in tents and keep livestock." Jabal is identified as the progenitor of pastoral nomadism — the same livelihood as Abel. The Cainite line, marked by the murder of the shepherd, ironically generates the shepherd-culture. ʾĀbî (father of) functions as "founder of" or "originator of" a cultural tradition; this is a genealogical formula for the origin of human cultural specializations.
21: ʾābî kol-tōpēś kinnôr wĕʿûgāb: "The father of all who play the lyre and pipe." Musical instruments are attributed to the Cainite line — kinnôr (lyre/harp, later David's instrument; H3658) and ʿûgāb (pipe/flute; H5748). The attribution of music, livestock, and metalworking to Cain's descendants reflects an ANE tradition of tracing cultural achievements to legendary ancestors. In context it is morally neutral description rather than condemnation; human civilization — art, agriculture, technology — develops from the fallen line.
22: tûbal-qayin lōṭēš kol-ḥōrēš nĕḥōšet ûbarzel: "Tubal-Cain, the forger of all instruments of bronze and iron." Lōṭēš (H3913, sharpen/forge) is a technical smithing term. Bronze (nĕḥōšet) and iron (barzel) represent the metals of weapon-making in ANE contexts; the Cainite line produces pastoral culture (Jabal), musical culture (Jubal), and weapons technology (Tubal-Cain) — a complete civilization arising from the cursed line.
23: šimʿan qôlî nĕšê lemek: The Song of the Sword (vv23-24) is among the oldest poetic fragments in Genesis, recognizable by its parallel stichoi and archaic style. Lamech's boast escalates Cain's protection sevenfold to Lamech's self-proclaimed seventy-sevenfold vengeance. Hāragtî ʾîš lĕpitʿî: "I have killed a man for wounding me" — the verb hārag (killed) is the same used of Cain's murder in v8; Lamech celebrates rather than laments. The poem shows the degenerative spiral: sin's entry (ch. 3) → fratricidal murder (v8) → institutionalized revenge culture (vv23-24).
24: kî šibʿātayim yuqqam qayin wĕlemek šibʿîm wĕšibʿāh: "If Cain is avenged sevenfold, then Lamech seventy-sevenfold." The escalation from seven to seventy-seven is arithmetically exact (seven multiplied to the second level of completeness). Jesus reverses this exact calculus in Matt 18:22: "Not seven times, but seventy-seven times" — forgiveness is to escalate by the same magnitude that revenge did. The NT is reading the Lamech poem when it sets the counter-principle of unlimited forgiveness against unlimited retaliation.
25: wayyāšet lî ʾĕlōhîm zeraʿ ʾaḥēr taḥat hābel: "God has appointed for me another offspring in place of Abel." The name Šēt (Seth, H8352) is explained by Eve as deriving from šāt (H7896, to appoint/set) — God has set/appointed a replacement. This is a theological naming that sees Seth not as a genetic replacement but as a covenantal appointment — the line through which the seed-promise of 3:15 will continue. The verb šātat (appointed) contrasts with the name Cain (acquired/purchased): Seth is gift, not achievement.
26: ʾāz hūḥal liqrōʾ bĕšēm YHWH: "Then people began to call on the name of YHWH." Hūḥal (from ḥālal, H2490) is rendered variously as "began" or "was profaned" depending on whether it is read as Hophal of yāḥal (began) or Hophal of ḥālal (to profane). If the latter, the verse is ironic — worship of YHWH's name began, but it was already being profaned by the Cainite culture. The majority reading is "began" (worship started with the birth of Enosh). Šēm YHWH (the name of YHWH) — invoking the divine name is a liturgical act; this is the first explicit reference to communal worship in Scripture, located not at a shrine but in the context of Enosh's birth, whose name (ʾĕnôš, H583) means "mortal/weak human."
